hook.scenario: You search your docs for 'how to cancel subscription'. The vector database returns 20 chunks. The actual instructions for cancellation are at position #14. But because you only feed the top-5 chunks to the LLM (to save tokens), the model never sees the answer.
hook.question: Why did the vector database rank the right answer so poorly, and how do we fix it?

mental_model.intuition: Vector search is like scanning a massive library using a metal detector—it's fast but imprecise. Reranking is having an expert sit down with the 20 items the metal detector found and carefully order them by true relevance.
mental_model.description: Standard embeddings compress a whole paragraph into a single point in space. This compression loses nuance. So 'fast' vector search (bi-encoders) often misses the subtle differences between a good match and a great match. To fix this, we do a two-stage retrieval: first, we use fast vector search to fetch the top 50 'okay' results. Then, we pass those 50 results to a specialized Reranker model (a cross-encoder) that painstakingly scores how well each chunk answers the specific query, pushing the truly relevant chunks to the very top.

sections.body: Vector similarity is incredibly fast because it's pre-computed. We embed the chunks once, then just calculate distance at query time. But this speed comes with a massive penalty: standard embeddings are 'bi-encoders'. They embed the query and the chunk in total isolation. They don't know they are meant for each other.
sections.recap: Fast vector search embeds query and chunk separately, which often misses nuanced relevance.

sections.body: Because of this isolation, vector search frequently ranks a highly relevant chunk at position #15 instead of #1. If your RAG prompt only takes the top 5 chunks to stay under the token limit, the LLM will miss the answer entirely. We need a way to reorder these results.

image.alt: Two lists: similarity order mixes relevant and not; after reranking the relevant chunks are on top.

sections.recap: If the real answer is ranked #15, a top-5 RAG pipeline fails.

sections.body: Enter the Reranker (usually a 'cross-encoder'). Unlike a standard embedding model, a cross-encoder takes BOTH the query and the chunk at the exact same time. It reads them together, allowing it to understand deep semantic interactions. It outputs a highly accurate relevance score from 0 to 1.
sections.recap: Cross-encoders read the query and chunk together to produce a highly accurate relevance score.

sections.body: If cross-encoders are so accurate, why not use them for the whole database? Because they are excruciatingly slow. Running a cross-encoder against 1 million chunks would take minutes per query. Vector search takes milliseconds. So we combine them: speed first, accuracy second.
sections.recap: Cross-encoders are too slow for millions of documents, so we only use them on the final shortlist.

sections.body: The two-stage pipeline: Step 1: Use fast vector search to retrieve a wide net of 50 chunks. Step 2: Pass those 50 chunks to the reranker. Step 3: Sort by the reranker's score. Step 4: Feed the new top-5 into the LLM. This gives you the speed of vector search with the precision of a cross-encoder.
sections.recap: Pipeline: Query → Fast Vector Search (top 50) → Rerank (top 5) → LLM Prompt.

code_snippets.title: The Cohere Rerank API (Two-Stage Retrieval)
code_snippets.code:
import cohere

co = cohere.Client("YOUR_API_KEY")
query = "How to cancel subscription"

# Stage 1: Fast vector search returns 20 okay-ish chunks
# (Assume `vector_db.search` returns a list of text strings)
initial_results = vector_db.search(query, k=20)

# Stage 2: Rerank those 20 chunks for deep relevance
rerank_response = co.rerank(
    model="rerank-english-v3.0",
    query=query,
    documents=initial_results,
    top_n=3  # Only keep the absolute best 3 for the LLM prompt
)

# The final list is now perfectly ordered for the LLM
final_chunks = [initial_results[result.index] for result in rerank_response.results]
code_snippets.explanation: We don't rerank the entire database. We fetch a medium-sized list (20) using fast vector search, then use the specialized reranker to bubble the top 3 to the surface.

key_points.title: Nearest ≠ Relevant
key_points.detail: A vector database returns the geometrically closest points, which are not always the logically most relevant answers to a question.

key_points.title: Bi-encoder vs Cross-encoder
key_points.detail: Vector search uses bi-encoders (fast, isolated). Reranking uses cross-encoders (slow, simultaneous, accurate).

key_points.title: Two-Stage Retrieval
key_points.detail: Always use both. Vector search filters millions down to 50. Reranking filters 50 down to the 5 you actually send to the LLM.

key_points.title: Cost and Latency
key_points.detail: Reranking adds a network hop and latency (often ~100-200ms). The accuracy boost is usually worth it.

common_mistakes.title: Trying to rerank the whole database
common_mistakes.explanation: You cannot run a cross-encoder against 100,000 documents at query time. It must only be used on a small shortlist (top 50-100) returned by a fast vector search.

common_mistakes.title: Using reranking when initial retrieval fails entirely
common_mistakes.explanation: Reranking only reorders the items it is given. If the vector search missed the relevant document completely (e.g. it was ranked #500 and you only fetched top-20), the reranker cannot magically find it.

recall_questions.q: Why is vector search (bi-encoder) less accurate than a reranker (cross-encoder)?
recall_questions.answer: Vector search embeds the query and chunk in isolation. A cross-encoder reads both at the same time, allowing it to understand the subtle relationship between the specific question and the text.

recall_questions.q: If rerankers are more accurate, why don't we use them instead of vector databases?
recall_questions.answer: They are computationally expensive and slow. Running a cross-encoder against millions of documents would take minutes, whereas vector math takes milliseconds.
First, use fast vector search to retrieve a broad set of results (e.g. top 50). Second, pass that shortlist through a reranker to reorder them, keeping only the top 5 for the LLM.

oa_questions.question: Your RAG pipeline fetches 5 chunks from the vector database. The LLM's answer is wrong, but you manually verify that the correct chunk exists in the database at position #15. You cannot increase the LLM context window. How do you fix this?
oa_questions.answer: Implement two-stage retrieval. Expand the initial vector search to fetch 20-50 chunks, then pass those chunks to a reranker model. The reranker will score them for deep relevance and bubble the correct chunk from #15 into the top 5, allowing it to fit in the context window.
oa_questions.approach: Identify that the vector search is returning the document, just ranked too low. Reranking is the canonical solution for the 'right doc, wrong position' problem.

oa_questions.question: You are designing a low-latency Q&A system. A colleague suggests using a cross-encoder to directly search the 500,000 document corpus to maximize accuracy. Why is this a bad idea?
oa_questions.answer: A cross-encoder must evaluate the query against every single document individually at query time. Running 500,000 deep neural network passes sequentially will take minutes or hours. It violates the low-latency requirement.
oa_questions.approach: Highlight the catastrophic performance impact of running a cross-encoder at scale. Emphasize that cross-encoders must be gated behind a fast first-stage retrieval.
